s2.q: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is defined as:
options: Carbohydrate intolerance of variable severity with onset during pregnancy
s2.explain: GDM is carbohydrate intolerance of variable severity with onset or first recognition during pregnancy.

s3.q: White's classification of diabetes in pregnancy includes all EXCEPT:
s3.explain: White's classification includes A (GDM), B (onset >20, duration <10 yrs), C (onset 10-19, duration 10-19 yrs), D (>20 yrs duration), F (nephropathy), R (retinopathy), H (heart disease).

s4.q: During pregnancy, the physiologic change that increases risk for GDM is:
options: Increased insulin resistance from placental hormones (hPL, cortisol)
s4.explain: Placental hormones (hPL, estrogen, progesterone, cortisol) cause increasing insulin resistance as pregnancy advances.

s6.q: Effects of diabetes on pregnancy include all EXCEPT:
s6.explain: DM in pregnancy is associated with macrosomia (not IUGR), polyhydramnios, congenital anomalies, and preeclampsia.

s6.q: A classic congenital anomaly associated with pregestational DM is:
s6.explain: Sacral agenesis (caudal regression syndrome) is a classic anomaly associated with pregestational diabetes.

s7.q: Risk factors for GDM include all EXCEPT:
s7.explain: Risk factors: age >35, obesity, previous GDM, family history of DM, and high-risk ethnicity.

s8.q: Screening for GDM is performed with:
s8.explain: Universal screening with GCT (50g glucose, 1-hour plasma glucose) at 24-28 weeks. Abnormal result is followed by diagnostic GTT.

s9.q: First-line treatment for gestational diabetes is:
options: Medical nutrition therapy and lifestyle modification
s9.explain: MNT and lifestyle modification with blood glucose monitoring is first-line. Insulin is added if glycemic targets are not met.

options: OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum and lifestyle counseling
s12.explain: OGTT should be performed at 6-12 weeks postpartum to reclassify glucose tolerance, with lifestyle modification and surveillance.
